texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide levels have been linked to climate change [1], which is associated with the world's dependence on fossil fuels. One viable option in reducing carbon emissions is the capture of carbon dioxide from large point sources such as power plants, with long term storage through geosequestration, known as carbon capture and storage [2]. Membrane technology holds a number of advantages for CO2 capture [3,4]. The approach is operationally simple; the removal of gas through a selective film ensures high energy efficiency; and the equipment footprint is small leading to lower capital cost compared to conventional separation processes.

Membrane technology holds a number of advantages for CO2 capture [3,4]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pre-combustion carbon capture separates carbon dioxide from syngas before combustion. Rubbery polymeric membranes offer the ability to separate carbon dioxide from syngas, and in particular hydrogen, with reasonable selectivity. Here, the gas separation performance of three rubbery polymeric membranes, poly dimethyl siloxane (PDMS), cross-linked polyethylene glycol (PEG) and poly (ether-b-amide) (PEBAX 2533), are reported upon exposure to real unshifted syngas, as part of the CO2CRC Mulgrave capture project. At low temperatures, all three membranes were able to separate CO2 from syngas; however competitive sorption by other syngas components significantly altered their performance relative to reported laboratory results. For PDMS, under syngas conditions CO2 permeability was reduced compared to the pure gas permeability, but CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 selectivity were improved in the presence of syngas. This resulted in good CO2 separation performance for this membrane. However over 24 h of exposure the performance decreased somewhat. In contrast, both PEG and PEBAX membranes demonstrated poor CO2 separation performance upon exposure to syngas. In particular, low CO2/H2 selectivities where observed, indicating that both membranes upon exposure to syngas had poor selective ability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pre-combustion processes can be further classified into those that use oxygen-blown gasification and those that use an air-blown gasifier. In the former case, the shifted syngas is as above; a fairly simple mixture of CO2 and H2. In such circumstances, a hydrogenselective membrane is usually most appropriate, as the carbon dioxide can be retained at a high pressure for storage and a sweep gas can be used on the permeate side to ensure low permeate side partial pressures [6]. However, in air-blown gasification the syngas is diluted by a significant stream of nitrogen, complicating the carbon dioxide capture operation. In such operations, it may become more economic to use a CO2-selective membrane, permeating carbon dioxide while retaining a hydrogen/nitrogen mixture which is suitable for direct combustion in a downstream turbine.

texts.orig: Hydrogen is a small molecule and so is readily separated from the other syngas components using a size-selective membrane. However the development of membranes that will instead retain the small molecular size of H2 but permeate the larger CO2 is a significant challenge [7] and can only be achieved through polymeric membranes [8]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, we report on the performance of three rubbery polymeric membranes, poly dimethyl siloxane (PDMS), cross-linked poly ethylene glycol (PEG) and poly ether-b-amide (PEBAX 2533), in pre-combustion carbon capture pilot plant trials. These trials were part of the Cooperative Research Centre for Greenhouse Gas Technologies (CO2CRC) Mulgrave capture project [9,10]. The rubbery polymeric membranes are operated to separate CO2 from unshifted syngas, generated from air-blown brown coal gasification. Our aim was to determine the membrane and strategy that has the most potential for successful implementation of carbon capture on an industrial scale [11]. The polymeric membranes, PDMS, PEG and PEBAX 2533 were chosen because of strong support within the literature for these polymers in CO2 separation from H2, based on laboratory results [7,12-15]. However, little research has focused on their performance under mixed gas conditions. Our own work has considered the impact of H2S, CO and water on CO2 permeation in PDMS [16] while Potreck et al. [17] and Sijbesma et al. [18] considered the impact of water on nitrogen permeation in PEBAX 1074. No information has been reported for their performance in actual syngas separation. This information is vital in the development of membrane technology for pre-combustion capture, to enable future membrane based capture plants to be designed and commissioned.

texts.orig: The membrane pilot plant was housed within a custom built cabinet (Erntec). All piping was 1/4 ′′ stainless steel tube, except for the main feed line from the gasifier which was 1/2 ′′ stainless steel tube, and the final exit stream to the onsite extraction unit (Nylon). All valves and fittings were made from stainless steel (Swagelok). Pressure gauges (Swagelok) and pressure transmitters (GEMS Sensors and Control -Basingstoke England) with displays (PR electronics) where positioned on all lines. A backpressure regulator (Porter 08011) on the retentate of the membrane unit controlled the pressure within the module, with feed gas flowrate measured by a gas rotameter (0-10 L/min Kytala (Muurame Finland)). texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed syngas was generated by air-blown gasification of Victorian brown coal, without water gas shift. The feed is taken from the gasification process after being flashed and enters the pilot plant at ambient temperature (cooled by the connecting pipework between the gasifier and membrane pilot plant) and 8.2 ± 0.1 bar absolute pressure. Coal dust and larger organics are separated through a separator vessel and filter, before reheating to the permeation test temperature. The average composition of the syngas is displayed in Table 1, where the heavy hydrocarbons are a mixture of ethylene, ethane, propylene and propane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In pilot plant experiments, the membranes were initially exposed to N2 at pressure to ensure temperature equilibrium and to purge O2 from the lines. The membranes were then exposed to syngas and operated for 3-4 h at temperature, during which time gas analysis of all lines was undertaken. For long time studies, the membranes were operated for 6 h at a time, and stored under N2 in situ overnight, before being exposed to syngas again. This stop-start process was due to limitations in the gasifier operational period. It was not possible to analyse the gas samples for their water content and hence the water balance around each membrane was approximated by assuming the water permeability determined for each membrane under pure gas conditions is retained (Table 2), with temperature dependence proportional to that observed for CO2 permeability in each membrane. While very high water permeabilities are used, the low water content of the feed gas means that the actual water flux is small and so the errors introduced by this approximation are minimal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pure gas permeabilities of syngas components through PDMS, PEG and PEBAX 2533 and the ideal selectivity relative to CO2 for each membrane determined from these results is reported in Table 2.
texts.text: The pure gas permeabilities of syngas components through PDMS, PEG and PEBAX 2533 and the ideal selectivity relative to CO2 for each membrane determined from these results is reported in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For PEG, the H2 and N2 permeabilities match literature values (17 and 2 Barrer respectively [24,25]), while the CO2 permeability is half that reported in the literature (130 Barrer). This suggests that the level of crystallinity has not been reduced to the same extent as reported by Lin et al. [24]. This is associated with the level of cross-linking and the difference in the acrylic length of the original polymer. However, this permeability is five times greater than that for pure PEO of 12 Barrer [14]. For PEBAX 2533, comparison with literature is difficult because of the widely reported permeability values, shown in Table 3. Bondar et al. [26] reports permeability of CO2, H2 and N2, which are roughly double those reported here for the same membrane synthesis, but the selectivities reported are similar to those reported here, CO2/H2 is 5.1 and CO2/N2 is 22. Likewise, Barbi et al. [27] and Tocci et al. [28] report permeabilities similar to Bondar et al. However, Gugliuzza and Drioli [29] report much higher peremabilities for CO2 and N2, along with Car et al. [30], but have similar CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 selectivities to the rest of the literature and here. Similarly, Chen et al. [31] reports similar CO2/N2 selectivity for PEBAX 2533 as reported here. However, Nguyen et al. [32] reports lower CO2 permeabilities for a wide range of PEBAX grades, (i.e. PEBAX 3000 is 45.8 Barrer). This variation in PEBAX permeabilities indicates that the membrane casting procedure has an important influence on the underlying morphology of the polymer, which influences the relative permeabilities of each gas. Importantly, these variations have less influence on the CO2 selectivity against other gases. The measured CO2, H2 and N2 permeability for PDMS are very similar to that reported in the literature [33].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the pure gas laboratory results and the corresponding literature data, PDMS displays the highest gas permeability, compared to both PEG and PEBAX membranes, with reasonable CO2/H2 selectivity, meaning it is favourable for separation of CO2 from H2. However, the presence of N2 coupled with the low CO2/N2 selectivity of PDMS means greater quantities of N2 will exist in the permeate stream. In comparison, PEG and PEBAX have reasonable CO2/H2 selectivity and high CO2/N2 selectivity ensuring good permeate purity, but low CO2 permeability meaning more membrane area will be required to achieve a similar amount of CO2 recovery as PDMS.

texts.orig: There is a sizeable reduction in H2 and N2 relative to the pure gas permeability, which may be partly attributed to competition from CO2, because of the strong solubility affinity in PDMS. However, the N2 permeability is considerably less than that reported by our own group for a CO2-N2 mixed gas system, and for mixtures of CO2-N2 with either water, CO or H2S [16]. The additional reduction may relate to the presence of heavy hydrocarbons in the gas stream but this requires further investigation. The CO, N2 and H2 permeability results are also all considerably lower than those reported by Merkel et al. [37] for a simulated syngas. However this simulated syngas contained much greater quantities of H2S (1.5 mol% versus around 0.05 mol%) and no heavy hydrocarbons. We have previously shown that H2S can cause an increase in the permeabilities of inert gases such as N2 through plasticisation, while lowering the permeability of CO2 through competitive sorption [16].

texts.orig: Under syngas conditions, PDMS demonstrates reasonable CO2 selectivity (permeability ratio) against the major components in syngas, especially at low temperatures. The CO2/H2 selectivity is little changed from the pure gas value at 35 ◦ C, but a little higher than the value of 3.5 achieved by Barillas et al. [36] in their CO2/H2/Ar mixture. However, a considerably better CO2/N2 permeability ratio is achieved than observed in the pure gas case at 35 ◦ C (Table 2). This is achieved because of the substantial reduction in N2 permeability. In addition, reasonably good CO2/CO permeability ratio is observed, ∼ 11, for low temperatures. Hence, upon exposure to syngas, PDMS retains the ability to selectively separate CO2 against all major gas components, excluding H2O.

texts.orig: The increase in all gas permeabilities with temperature is attributed to increases in the diffusion coefficient of each gas through the polymeric matrix with temperature. As has been noted by Merkel et al. [37] as temperature increases, highly soluble species such as carbon dioxide increase in permeability more slowly than less soluble species such as hydrogen. This trend reflects the decline in the solubility coefficient that occurs simultaneously as temperature is increased. This results in a declining CO2 selectivity with temperature. While our data differs significantly to the results for the simulated syngas used by Merkel et al. [37] at 35 ◦ C (as discussed above), in both cases there is a similar declining trend in selectivity with temperature, with the CO2/H2 selectivity falling to unity between 120 and 170 ◦ C.

texts.orig: The permeability of CH4 through PEBAX 2533 at 50 ◦ C was determined to be ∼ 4 Barrer, which is lower than the pure gas permeability of 20 Barrer. The reduction can again be attributed to competitive sorption effects. Heavier hydrocarbons were not detected in the permeate stream, suggesting that their permeability through PEBAX is very low. Hence, from the performance under syngas conditions PEBAX 2533 is a poor membrane at separating CO2 from syngas, because of the high competitive sorption, reducing the capacity for CO2 to sorb within the membrane; coupled with membrane swelling that increases permeabilities of all gases. These two effects combined to decrease CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 selectivities, even at low temperatures. Similar to PDMS, the determined water flux through PEBAX 2533 under syngas conditions, at all temperatures, was evaluated to be less than 1% of the total gas flux through the membrane. Again, this is because of the low partial pressure in the feed (0.11 mol%) resulting in only a small driving force and further supports the water balance assumption is not a critical source of error in the determination of syngas components permeability through these rubbery polymeric membranes.

texts.orig: PDMS clearly demonstrates the best performance, in terms of both permeability and CO2/H2 selectivity compared to both PEBAX and PEG. Indeed, the overall performance of PDMS is not negatively affected by operating under syngas conditions, which is a contrast to PEG and PEBAX whose performance deteriorates sharply upon exposure to syngas. The most likely reason for this difference is the difference in chemistry of the three polymers, with PDMS being based on siloxane groups, which would appear to be more resilient to conditions, compared to the ether groups in both PEG and PEBAX.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation of CO2 from syngas, and in particular H2, requires rubbery membranes that are solubility selective. Here, three rubbery membranes, PDMS, PEBAX 2533 and cross-linked PEG are trialled in the CO2CRC Mulgrave capture project membrane pilot plant. The syngas influences the performance of all three membranes. In most cases, substantial decreases in CO2 permeability are observed, relative to the ideal laboratory results, as a result of competitive sorption from other gas components. For the hydrophobic polymer, PDMS, the permeability of other gas species also decline, so that the net changes in the CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 permeability ratios are not substantially affected. Conversely, for the hydrophilic polymers, PEBAX and PEG, the solubility of hydrogen and nitrogen increase relative to the laboratory results, reflecting morphology change. This leads to a significant loss in CO2/H2 selectivity. Results suggest that only PDMS should be considered further as a membrane material for the separation of CO2 from syngas generation from air-blown gasification.
texts.text: Membrane separation of CO2 from syngas, and in particular H2, requires rubbery membranes that are solubility selective. Here, three rubbery membranes, PDMS, PEBAX 2533 and cross-linked PEG are trialled in the CO2CRC Mulgrave capture project membrane pilot plant. The syngas influences the performance of all three membranes. In most cases, substantial decreases in CO2 permeability are observed, relative to the ideal laboratory results, as a result of competitive sorption from other gas components. For the hydrophobic polymer, PDMS, the permeability of other gas species also decline, so that the net changes in the CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 permeability ratios are not substantially affected. Conversely, for the hydrophilic polymers, PEBAX and PEG, the solubility of hydrogen and nitrogen increase relative to the laboratory results, reflecting morphology change. This leads to a significant loss in CO2/H2 selectivity. Results suggest that only PDMS should be considered further as a membrane material for the separation of CO2 from syngas generation from air-blown gasification.
